---
title: "Second SSD Error"
date: 2023-12-30
forum: General Help
---

### Post by shorty-stu on 2023-12-30
Hi
I have 2 SSD's on my PC with the first as the boot drive with Windows and Ubuntu.
The second drive was opening and I could read and write to it.
I changed the partition ans since then I can not open the drive "DiskD12"
I get the following message 
[LIST]
[*]'Could Not Display "DiskD12" '
[*]'The file is of an unknown type'
[/LIST]
I can see the drive in both Files and I have re-partitioned, re-formatted it in Disks but nothing works.
The drive is mounted in   /media/stuart/DiskD12 is this the correct location?

Any help on this would be greatly appreciated.

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-30
Post the output of **sudo fdisk -l** if you'd like any help. Wrap that output in forum code tags, since columns matter.

Nobody can say that  /media/stuart/DiskD12 is correct or incorrect. That's up to you, but I wouldn't mount anything there and I 'd only mount using the **fstab** or **autofs** to control the mount location and options.  But some think I'm weird and a bit of a control freak.

SSDs do fail.  If there is any issue with an SSD, my first assumption would be that it is failing and that I need to get all precious data off it, ASAP.  I've had 2 SSDs fail. Sometimes they fail with ZERO warning and sometimes we can have a month of warning.  Most of the time, there isn't any warning and they just fail.  All storage fails - sometimes after 1 day and sometimes after 15 yrs. No way to tell with SSDs.

Oh, and I don't trust Gnome-Disks for anything.  Gparted or fdisk or parted are my tools of choice. Just depends on what is needed.

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-30
No need to attach separate files.

You should select+paste the terminal output **AS TEXT** into this forum like any other text you'd type.

Then you should use the code-tags button like you'd use bold/italics/underline/quote buttons in the advanced editor. No images.  The code-tags button is the button with a '#' on it.

[Https://ubuntuforums.org/misc.php?do=bbcode#code](Https://ubuntuforums.org/misc.php?do=bbcode#code) has more details, if you need them.

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2023-12-31
The media directory or folder is under root and not under home/username/ as you have mounted it. We can use the media directory or the mnt directory.

Regards

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-31
```
Disk /dev/nvme0n1: 953.87 GiB, 1024209543168 bytes, 2000409264 sectors
Disk model: SAMSUNG MZVL21T0HCLR-00BL2              
Units: sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytes
Sector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
I/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
Disklabel type: gpt
Disk identifier: 70CC0711-B0EB-4E95-BDFA-8922E5A63EF5

Device              Start        End    Sectors   Size Type
/dev/nvme0n1p1       2048     534527     532480   260M EFI System
/dev/nvme0n1p2     534528     567295      32768    16M Microsoft reserved
/dev/nvme0n1p3     567296  562712575  562145280 268.1G Microsoft basic data
/dev/nvme0n1p4  562712576 1996310527 1433597952 683.6G Microsoft basic data
/dev/nvme0n1p5 1996312576 2000408575    4096000     2G Windows recovery environm


Disk /dev/nvme1n1: 953.87 GiB, 1024209543168 bytes, 2000409264 sectors
Disk model: SAMSUNG MZVL21T0HCLR-00BL2              
Units: sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytes
Sector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
I/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
Disklabel type: gpt
Disk identifier: 4CA5E8A5-E277-4726-9A7A-7327BF171933

Device              Start        End    Sectors   Size Type
/dev/nvme1n1p1 1395539968 2000408575  604868608 288.4G Microsoft basic data
/dev/nvme1n1p2       2048 1395539967 1395537920 665.4G **Linux filesystem**

Partition table entries are not in disk order.

```
Everything else is junk.  There's only 1 linux file system according to that output and the partition numbers are out of order (which doesn't matter, just odd).

The first step I'd try is to run an fsck on the Linux file system,
```
sudo fsck -y /dev/nvme1**n1p2**
```
You'll likely need to boot off Linux installation media in "Try Ubuntu" mode to make this possible. Running fsck cannot be done on a file system that is "open" or "used".  Or you might be able to boot into recovery mode under the Grub Advanced menu and run fsck against all file systems. This would be easier, if it works.

Do that and report back.  I'm not used to seeing Linux installs with just 1 partition. Is that even possible?

---

### Post by tea for one on 2023-12-31
> **shorty-stu said:**
> Hi
I have 2 SSD's on my PC with the first as the boot drive with Windows and Ubuntu.
Your first disk only contains Windows partitions. Ubuntu is not visible there.
```
Device Start End Sectors Size Type
/dev/nvme0n1p1 2048 534527 532480 260M EFI System
/dev/nvme0n1p2 534528 567295 32768 16M Microsoft reserved
/dev/nvme0n1p3 567296 562712575 562145280 268.1G Microsoft basic data
/dev/nvme0n1p4 562712576 1996310527 1433597952 683.6G Microsoft basic data
/dev/nvme0n1p5 1996312576 2000408575 4096000 2G Windows recovery environm
```

Edit: Boot drive - Do you mean the ESP contains Ubuntu boot files?

---

### Post by shorty-stu on 2023-12-31
Hi thanks for getting back to me on this.
I don't fully understand your comment, is the mount in the wrong place? I did not specify the location 'Disk' assigned the location. What is the best way to do this?
I'm not sure of the directory structure but under Administrator Root I have a 'media' directory as well as a 'mnt' directory.
in the 'media' directory is the mount "/media/stuart/DiskD12" is it incorrect to have the /stuart/ sub-directory?
'mnt' directory is empty

---

### Post by tea for one on 2023-12-31
I think that I've got it now.
DiskD12 - Is it partition /dev/nvme1n1p1?

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-31
> **shorty-stu said:**
> Hi Thanks for your help with this.
Apologies not used forums before is this what you mean?
I forgot to mention the SSD's 2 x 1TB are fixed not removable.

No. 

I posted what I meant above. See how the columns all line up?  Posting terminal output in that way is the standard for these forums.  Makes it easier to help you.  All the "loop" devices are useless - just cruft and seldom, if ever, needed an any posts. Safe to NEVER show loop devices unless that is specifically what the question is about, which would happen perhaps 1 in 100,000 questions.

But the output doesn't show what you intend. I have no idea which file system you want to mount.  Nor where you'd like it mounted. DiskD12 isn't shown clearly.

---

### Post by shorty-stu on 2023-12-31
Hi thanks for your help, i defiantly have a Linux boot on the nvme0 device it is labeled Microsoft basic data and is the 683.6G partition. In 'Disk' it is shown as 734GB Ext4 mounted at 'Filesystem Root'.
  
>  Device              Start        End    Sectors   Size Type
/dev/nvme0n1p1       2048     534527     532480   260M EFI System
/dev/nvme0n1p2     534528     567295      32768    16M Microsoft reserved
/dev/nvme0n1p3     567296  562712575  562145280 268.1G Microsoft basic data
[highlight]/dev/nvme0n1p4  562712576 1996310527 1433597952 683.6G Microsoft basic data[/highlight]
/dev/nvme0n1p5 1996312576 2000408575    4096000     2G Windows recovery environm

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-31
Run this command and post the output, using 'code-tags' - not quotes. Quotes are useless when columns are needed.
```
lsblk -e 7 -o name,type,fstype,size,FSAVAIL,FSUSE%,label,mountpoint
```

---

### Post by shorty-stu on 2023-12-31
Thanks for the heads-up will remember this in future.

See below 
```
 NAME        TYPE FSTYPE   SIZE FSAVAIL FSUSE% LABEL       MOUNTPOINT
nvme0n1     disk        953.9G                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p1 part vfat     260M  212.3M    17% SYSTEM_DRV  /boot/efi
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p2 part           16M                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p3 part ntfs   268.1G                Windows-SSD 
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p4 part ext4   683.6G    577G     9%             /
&#9492;&#9472;nvme0n1p5 part ntfs       2G                WINRE_DRV   
nvme1n1     disk        953.9G                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme1n1p1 part vfat   288.4G                            
&#9492;&#9472;nvme1n1p2 part ext4   665.4G  620.6G     0% DriveD12    /media/stuart/Drive2
```

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-31
You can edit old posts. That is preferred for little format mistakes.

Anyway, it took a bunch of posts to just get the information.  What was the question again?  Did you already run **fsck** against all the Linux file systems?  Do that as step 1.

Some of the output in post #16 is suspect. Data shown and mount locations don't match what would happen.

This could account for any discrepancy.

### Post by shorty-stu on 2023-12-31
Ok so here is the output from
 &#8216;sudo fsck -y /dev/nvme1n1p2&#8217;
 
 
 fsck from util-linux 2.39.1
 e2fsck 1.47.0 (5-Feb-2023)
 ext2fs_open2: Bad magic number in super-block
 fsck.ext2: Superblock invalid, trying backup blocks&#8230;
 fsck.ext2: Bad magic number in super-block while trying to open /dev/nvme1n1p2
 
 
 The superblock could not be read or does not describe a valid ext2/ext3/ext4  
 filesystem. If the device is valid and it really contains an ext2/ext3/ext4  

 filesystem (and not swap or ufs or something else), then the superblock

 is corrupt, and you might try running e2fsck with an alternative superblock:
     e2fsck -b 8193 <device>
 or
     e2fsck -b 32768 <device>

I also tried it for /dev/nvme0n1p2
with the same result

---

### Post by TheFu on 2023-12-31
Did you ensure the partitions were unused, as spelled out above?  They cannot be mounted when **fsck** is run. 

nvme0n1p2 doesn't seem to have any file system on it, so fsck is useless. It is 16MB. Meh.  NTFS or vfat (fat32/exfat) don't usually support fsck. For those, use MS-Windows tools like chkdsk or scandisk.

The only partitions that could possibly be used with fdisk are
```
NAME        TYPE FSTYPE   SIZE FSAVAIL FSUSE% LABEL       MOUNTPOINT
nvme0n1     disk        953.9G                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p1 part vfat     260M  212.3M    17% SYSTEM_DRV  /boot/efi
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p2 part           16M                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p3 part ntfs   268.1G                Windows-SSD 
&#9500;&#9472;nvme0n1p4 part [COLOR="#FF0000"]ext4[/COLOR]   683.6G    577G     9%             /
&#9492;&#9472;nvme0n1p5 part ntfs       2G                WINRE_DRV   
nvme1n1     disk        953.9G                            
&#9500;&#9472;nvme1n1p1 part vfat   288.4G                            
&#9492;&#9472;nvme1n1p2 part [COLOR="#FF0000"]ext4[/COLOR]   665.4G  620.6G     0% DriveD12    /media/stuart/Drive2
```

Again, you'll need to boot into recovery mode or boot off alternate media - like a flash drive with the Ubuntu Installer on it, to ensure the partitions aren't in-use. Then run **fsck **.  Also, the device names can change from boot to boot, so always validate you know the correct partition with a native Linux file system before attempting to run fsck.

---
